1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning. Useful for a second opinion or a paper-style sketch.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Dispatch synchronously** (not via background `Bash` with `run_in_background: true`). Background dispatch leaves the parent's session log stuck on "running in background" on the dashboard.
45	
46	**Mandatory subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` MUST be dispatched at least once during the plan phase (similarly for `[review]`). The catalog tags them `[MANDATORY]`. A post-phase audit warns when a mandatory dispatch is missing. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no mandatory dispatch is required.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and clears the file after the plan phase succeeds. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).
133

1847	  Let $X_1, X_2$ be schemes over $\Spec k$, and let $U_1 \subseteq X_1$, $U_2 \subseteq X_2$ be affine open subsets with $U_1 = \Spec B_1$ and $U_2 = \Spec B_2$. Then the square of commutative ring maps
1848	  \[
1849	    \begin{array}{ccc}
1850	      k & \longrightarrow & B_1 \\
1851	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \\
1852	      B_2 & \longrightarrow & B
1853	    \end{array}
1854	  \]

1859	  \leanok
1860	  Three Mathlib facts chain to deliver the pushout structure:
1861	  \begin{enumerate}
1862	    \item \emph{Affine pullback identifies $W$ with $\Spec(B_1 \otimes_k B_2)$.} The Mathlib lemma \texttt{pullbackSpecIso} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Pullbacks}) supplies the canonical iso $\Spec(B_1 \otimes_k B_2) \cong \Spec B_1 \times_{\Spec k} \Spec B_2$ as schemes, together with the simp companions \texttt{pullbackSpecIso\_hom\_fst} / \texttt{\_hom\_snd} / \texttt{\_inv\_fst} / \texttt{\_inv\_snd} characterising its action against the two projections. Composing with the chart inclusion $W \hookrightarrow X_1 \times_k X_2$ identifies $\Gamma(X_1 \times_k X_2, W) \cong B_1 \otimes_k B_2$ as a $k$-algebra; since both $W$ and $\Spec(B_1 \otimes_k B_2)$ are affine and their structure-sheaf global sections agree, $W \cong \Spec(B_1 \otimes_k B_2)$ as schemes over $\Spec k$.
1863	    \item \emph{$\Spec$ sends ring pushout squares to scheme pullback squares.} The Mathlib lemma \texttt{isPullback\_SpecMap\_of\_isPushout} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Pullbacks}) gives the forward direction; in step 1 we already exhibited the pullback square on the scheme side, and the converse direction (a pullback of affine spectra over $\Spec k$ is the \texttt{Spec} of a $\mathtt{CommRingCat}$-pushout square of the underlying rings) follows by reading the iso of step 1 back through $\Spec$'s contravariant action on pushout/pullback squares.
1864	    \item \emph{Bridge from $\mathtt{CommRingCat}$-pushout to \texttt{Algebra.IsPushout}.} The lemma \texttt{CommRingCat.isPushout\_iff\_isPushout} (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.Ring.Pushout}) translates the $\mathtt{CommRingCat}$-pushout statement of step 2 into the \texttt{Algebra.IsPushout}\,$k$\,$B_1$\,$B_2$\,$B$ statement on the underlying $k$-algebras (the underlying class is \texttt{Algebra.IsPushout} from \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.IsTensorProduct}, the iter-145-verified Mathlib home for \texttt{Algebra.IsPushout} per the NOTE in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean:10--15}; an earlier blueprint draft cited the file as \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.IsPushout}, which does not exist upstream).

1872	[(ii-$\beta$-core) Per-chart translation-invariance K\"ahler-derivation: $\mathrm df = 0$ factors through the base field]
1873	  \label{lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart}
1874	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart}
1875	  \uses{lem:chart_algebra_isPushout_of_affine_product, lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero, lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}
1876	  Let $C : \Over\,(\Spec k)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$, let $A : \Over\,(\Spec k)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $k$ with identity element $\eta_A \in A(k)$, and let $f \colon C \to A$ be a morphism in $\Over\,(\Spec k)$ with $\mathrm df = 0$ (the scheme-level cotangent pull-back $f^{*}\,\Omega_{A/k} \to \Omega_{C/k}$ is the zero morphism of presheaves of $\mathcal O_C$-modules). Fix:
1877	  \begin{itemize}
1878	    \item an affine chart $W = \Spec B \subseteq A.\mathrm{left}$ around the image of $\eta_A$ on which $\Omega_{A/k}$ is presented as a free $B$-module of rank $g = \dim A$ via the standard-smooth presentation (Mathlib's \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential}), with chart generators $b_1, \ldots, b_g \in B$ whose universal differentials $\mathrm d b_1, \ldots, \mathrm d b_g$ form a $B$-basis of $\Omega_{B/k}$;
1879	    \item an affine chart $V = \Spec R \subseteq C.\mathrm{left}$ contained in $f^{-1}(W)$ on which the chart-restriction of $f$ yields a $k$-algebra map $f^{\sharp} \colon B \to R$.
1880	  \end{itemize}
1881	  Then for every $b \in B$,
1882	  \[
1883	    f^{\sharp}(b) \;\in\; \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,R) \;\subseteq\; R.
1884	  \]
1885	  Equivalently, the chart-level ring map $f^{\sharp} \colon B \to R$ factors as $B \to k \to R$.

The named theorem ``Serre duality on a smooth proper curve'' (piece (iv) of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}) is \emph{not} invoked as a packaged citation. Mathematically the content $H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = 0$ on a genus-$0$ curve coincides with the Serre dual of $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$, but the proof here runs the chart-\v Cech computation by hand on $\Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus g}$ using the same idiom that the project applies to compute $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$ in \texttt{Genus.lean}, rather than naming Serre duality. The ``without Serre duality'' disclaimer of the iter-144 chart-algebra envelope is therefore correct at the named-theorem level; it does \emph{not} mean ``without using $H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = 0$ at all'', and the proof must articulate the chart-\v Cech invocation explicitly (Step 3 below).

1891	  \uses{lem:chart_algebra_isPushout_of_affine_product, lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero, lem:constants_integral_over_base_field, thm:Scheme_AffineCoverMVSquare_HModule_prime_sequence_exact}

1893	  The proof proceeds in five steps. Steps 1--2 establish that the chart-level universal K\"ahler derivation $\mathrm dB \colon R \to \Omega_{R/k}$ satisfies $\mathrm dB(f^{\sharp}(b)) = 0$ for every $b \in B$, locally on a single affine chart $V$, from the hypothesis $\mathrm df = 0$ and the standard-smooth presentation of $\Omega_{A/k}$ on $W$. Step 3 promotes this chart-local kernel-vanishing to global section-vanishing on $C$ via the chart-\v Cech Mayer--Vietoris layer. Steps 4--5 extract the conclusion $f^{\sharp}(b) \in \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,R)$ via the ring-side helper (\cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero}) and the integrally-closed-constants helper (\cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}).
1894	
1895	  \emph{Step 1: chart-level translation of $\mathrm df = 0$.} The hypothesis $\mathrm df = 0$ on the chart pair $(W, V)$ is the statement that the induced map of $R$-modules
1896	  \[
1897	    R \,\otimes_B\, \Omega_{B/k} \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{R/k}, \qquad r \otimes \mathrm db \;\mapsto\; r \cdot \mathrm dB(f^{\sharp}(b)),
1898	  \]
1899	  is the zero map (the chart-level pullback $f^{\sharp,*}\,\Omega_{A/k} \to \Omega_{C/k}$, packaged via the algebra-side base change of K\"ahler modules along $f^{\sharp}$; here $\mathrm dB \colon R \to \Omega_{R/k}$ is the universal K\"ahler derivation of $R$ over $k$). Since $\Omega_{B/k}$ is a free $B$-module of rank $g$ with basis $\mathrm db_1, \ldots, \mathrm db_g$ via the standard-smooth presentation on $W$ (Mathlib's \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} and \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} of \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothCotangent}), the zero-map hypothesis unpacks as the system
1900	  \[
1901	    \mathrm dB(f^{\sharp}(b_i)) \;=\; 0 \qquad \text{in } \Omega_{R/k}, \qquad i = 1, \ldots, g.
1902	  \]
1903	
1904	  \emph{Step 2: extend to all $b \in B$.} For arbitrary $b \in B$, the universal K\"ahler derivation $\mathrm db \in \Omega_{B/k}$ has a $B$-linear expansion $\mathrm db = \sum_{i=1}^{g} c_i(b)\, \mathrm db_i$ with coefficients $c_i(b) \in B$ obtained from the standard-smooth presentation. The functoriality of the K\"ahler derivation along the ring map $f^{\sharp} \colon B \to R$ (Mathlib's \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.map} / \texttt{relativeDifferentials'\_map\_d}) gives $\mathrm dB(f^{\sharp}(b)) = \sum_{i=1}^{g} f^{\sharp}(c_i(b)) \cdot \mathrm dB(f^{\sharp}(b_i))$, which vanishes by Step 1. Hence
1905	  \[
1906	    \mathrm dB(f^{\sharp}(b)) \;=\; 0 \qquad \text{in } \Omega_{R/k}, \quad \text{for every } b \in B,
1907	  \]
1908	  as a chart-local statement on $V = \Spec R \subseteq C.\mathrm{left}$.
1909	
1910	  \emph{Step 3.aux: 2-chart affine cover of $C$ with affine intersection.} Step 3 below consumes a 2-chart affine cover $\{V_1, V_2\}$ of $C.\mathrm{left}$ with $V_1 \cap V_2$ also affine. Existence is standard for a smooth proper curve over $k$:
1911	  \begin{itemize}
1912	    \item $C$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated (proper $\Rightarrow$ both; Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}-namespace instances combined with \texttt{IsSeparated.quasiSeparated}).
1913	    \item Quasi-compactness plus the affine-cover API of \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} (the \texttt{IsAffineOpen} predicate and the \texttt{IsBasicOpen} / \texttt{LocalizationAway} family at \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Away.Basic}) supplies a finite affine cover $\{V_i\}_{i \in I}$ of $C.\mathrm{left}$ (via \texttt{Scheme.affineCover} together with \texttt{Scheme.affineCover\_isFinite} for $C$ Noetherian, available from quasi-compactness on a Noetherian base scheme).
1914	    \item Refining to a two-chart cover with affine intersection follows the construction of Stacks Tag~0F8L: pick any non-empty affine $V_1 \subseteq C.\mathrm{left}$; the complement $C \setminus V_1$ is a closed subset of $C.\mathrm{left}$ of codimension $\geq 1$, i.e.\ (since $C$ is one-dimensional) a finite set of closed points. A second affine open $V_2 \subseteq C.\mathrm{left}$ containing $C \setminus V_1$ exists by normality + irreducibility of $C$ (a smooth proper curve is normal; for any finite set of closed points one can find an affine neighbourhood, e.g.\ via the \texttt{AffineCover} construction plus the \texttt{Scheme.basicOpen\_of\_isAffineOpen}-style basic-open API). The intersection $V_1 \cap V_2 = V_1 \setminus (C \setminus V_2)$ is the complement in $V_1$ of a finite closed subset of $V_1$, hence a basic open of $V_1$ — i.e.\ $\Spec$ of a \texttt{LocalizationAway} construction on $\Gamma(C, V_1)$, hence affine.
1915	  \end{itemize}
1916	  If the project does not already expose a \texttt{Scheme.affineCover\_card\_two}-shape lemma specialised to smooth proper curves, the construction lands in tree as a thin wrapper around the \texttt{AffineCover} + \texttt{LocalizationAway} APIs; estimated build $\sim 20$--$40$ LOC.

1918	  \emph{Step 3: chart-\v Cech promotion to global section-vanishing on $C$.} The chart-local conclusion of Step 2 holds on the single chart $V \subseteq C.\mathrm{left}$ originally chosen in the lemma statement. 1920	  Concretely, the chart-\v Cech $\mathrm d^{0}$-complex of $\Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus g}$ on $\{V_1, V_2\}$ is the 2-term complex
1921	  \[
1922	    \mathrm d^{0} \colon\; \Gamma(V_1, \Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus g}) \,\oplus\, \Gamma(V_2, \Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus g}) \;\longrightarrow\; \Gamma(V_1 \cap V_2, \Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus g}), \qquad (\sigma_1, \sigma_2) \;\mapsto\; \sigma_1|_{V_1 \cap V_2} - \sigma_2|_{V_1 \cap V_2}.
1923	  \]
1924	  The chart-algebra ($\alpha$) helper \cref{lem:chart_algebra_isPushout_of_affine_product}, instantiated on the chart presentation of $V_1 \cap V_2$ as an affine product of $V_1$ and $V_2$ over the common overlap (in chart-algebra terms: the structure-sheaf rings sit in an \texttt{Algebra.IsPushout} square $k \to \Gamma(C, V_1)$ / $\Gamma(C, V_2) \to \Gamma(C, V_1 \cap V_2)$; the chart-algebra ($\alpha$) helper supplies this square at the ring level, which globalises to the standard sheaf-gluing pattern for an affine cover with affine intersection), identifies the kernel of $\mathrm d^{0}$ with the global sections via the standard sheaf-gluing pattern (a pair of chart sections agreeing on the intersection patches uniquely to a global section). Equivalently, the chart-side $\mathrm d^{0}$ identification with global-section-restriction is the $H^{0}$-row of the abstract Mayer--Vietoris sequence \cref{thm:Scheme_AffineCoverMVSquare_HModule_prime_sequence_exact} instantiated on the 2-chart cover with sheaf $\Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus g}$:
1925	  \[
1926	    0 \;\to\; \Gamma(C, \Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus g}) \;\to\; \Gamma(V_1, \Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus g}) \,\oplus\, \Gamma(V_2, \Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus g}) \;\xrightarrow{\mathrm d^{0}}\; \Gamma(V_1 \cap V_2, \Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus g}),
1927	  \]
1928	  so $\ker \mathrm d^{0} = \Gamma(C, \Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus g})$.

1930	  To verify the kernel element is zero, apply Steps 1--2 separately on each chart $V_i$ — taking $V := V_i$ in Step 1's chart-pair $(W, V)$ (chart-uniform existence is automatic: $A.\mathrm{hom}$ is smooth at the image of $\eta_A$, and the standard-smooth presentation on $W$ propagates to all preimages of $W$ in $C$ with the same generators $b_1, \ldots, b_g$; the chart-side ring map $f^{\sharp}_i \colon B \to \Gamma(C, V_i)$ for each $i$ then satisfies the chart-local kernel condition of Steps 1--2). Each chart-restriction $\mathrm dB(f^{\sharp}(b))|_{V_i} \in \Gamma(V_i, \Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus g})$ vanishes by Step 2's chart-local conclusion. The pair $(\mathrm dB(f^{\sharp}(b))|_{V_1}, \mathrm dB(f^{\sharp}(b))|_{V_2})$ lies in $\ker \mathrm d^{0}$ (automatic agreement on $V_1 \cap V_2$ by sheaf restriction) and equals the zero pair. Mathematically this content coincides with the Serre dual of $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C^{\oplus g}) = 0$; per the iter-145 strategy-critic Q3 honesty note in the statement block above, the proof here does \emph{not} invoke Serre duality as a packaged named theorem, but the underlying $H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = 0$ \emph{is} invoked (in the equivalent chart-\v Cech kernel form). 1935	  \[
1936	    f^{\sharp}(b) \;\in\; \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,R)
1937	  \]

1947	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1948	[(ii-$\beta$-aux) Constants of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible scheme are the base field]
1949	  \label{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}
1950	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.constants_integral_over_base_field}
1951	  Let $X$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible scheme over a field $k$, with structure morphism $\pi_X \colon X \to \Spec k$. Then the global sections form a finite-dimensional $k$-algebra that is integral over $k$ (via the canonical $\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ from $\pi_X$); and the image of $\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ coincides with $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$:
1952	  \[
1953	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) \;=\; \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)).
1954	  \]
1955	  Equivalently, $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) \cong k$ as $k$-algebras.
1956	\end{lemma}
1957	
1958	\begin{proof}
1959	
1960	\leanok
1961	  Three sub-facts chain to the conclusion:
1962	  \begin{enumerate}
1963	    \item \emph{$\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ is a finite-dimensional $k$-vector space.} For $X$ proper over $\Spec k$, the structure-sheaf pushforward $(\pi_X)_{*}\,\mathcal O_X$ is coherent on $\Spec k$ (a finitely-generated $k$-module), and its global sections are $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$. This is the standard properness-implies-finiteness-of-cohomology fact (Stacks Tag~0B5R / Hartshorne~II.5.19), available in Mathlib via the \texttt{IsProper}-namespace coherent-pushforward lemmas combined with \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.StructureSheaf} on global sections. As a corollary, $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ is a finite $k$-algebra.
1964	    \item \emph{$\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ is an integral domain.} $X$ is smooth (hence reduced) and geometrically irreducible over $k$, so $X$ is in particular irreducible and reduced, hence integral. The structure sheaf of an integral scheme has integral-domain global sections (Mathlib: \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsIntegral}-namespace lemmas on $\Gamma$).
1965	    \item \emph{A finite integral-domain $k$-algebra over a field is a finite field extension; geometric irreducibility forces dimension $1$.} Combining (1) and (2), $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ is a finite integral domain over the field $k$, hence is itself a field (a finite integral extension of a field is a field, Mathlib's \texttt{Algebra.IsIntegral.isField\_iff\_isField} in \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.IntegralClosure}), and a finite field extension of $k$. Geometric irreducibility of $X$ over $k$ propagates to $X_{\bar k} := X \times_k \Spec \bar k$ being integral and smooth proper over $\bar k$; therefore $\Gamma(X_{\bar k}, \mathcal O_{X_{\bar k}})$ is itself a finite field extension of $\bar k$, hence equals $\bar k$ (the algebraic closure has no proper finite extensions). The base-change identity $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) \otimes_k \bar k \cong \Gamma(X_{\bar k}, \mathcal O_{X_{\bar k}}) = \bar k$ (from flat base change of structure-sheaf global sections, Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsBaseChange}-namespace lemmas combined with $\Gamma$-functor) forces $\dim_k \Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) = 1$, hence $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) = k \cdot 1 = \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X))$.
1966	  \end{enumerate}
1967	  The three sub-facts deliver $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) \cong k$, i.e.\ the displayed equality. Estimated build: $\sim 50$--$100$ LOC.
1968

The integral-domain conclusion from \emph{integral} schemes uses Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsIntegral}-namespace lemmas on $\Gamma$ ([verified]). \texttt{Algebra.IsIntegral.isField\_iff\_isField} is [verified] in \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.IntegralClosure}. Flat base change of $\Gamma$ for a proper scheme is the standard Stacks Tag~02KH and is [verified in idiom] in Mathlib via the \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsBaseChange}-namespace; the project may need a [gap]-fill thin wrapper to state the chart-$\bar k$ identification cleanly. The geometric-irreducibility-to-integral step is direct from \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GeometricallyIrreducible} combined with the smoothness-implies-reducedness instance.
1970	\end{proof}

2043	\begin{theorem}\leanok
2044	[(ii-scheme-lift) Scheme-level $\mathrm df = 0$ implies $f$ factors through $\Spec k$]
2045	  \label{lem:Scheme_Over_ext_of_diff_zero}
2046	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_diff_zero}
2047	  \uses{lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart, thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
2048	  Let $f, g \colon C \to A$ be two morphisms in $\Over\,(\Spec k)$ between a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C$ of genus $0$ and a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $A$ over $k$, satisfying:
2049	  \begin{itemize}
2050	    \item $\mathrm df = \mathrm dg$ as morphisms of $\mathcal O_C$-modules $f^{*}\,\Omega_{A/k} \to \Omega_{C/k}$ (equivalently, the difference $\mathrm d(\mu \circ \langle f, \iota \circ g \rangle) = \mathrm df - \mathrm dg = 0$ vanishes, where $\mu$ and $\iota$ are the multiplication and inversion of $A$ supplied by the $\GrpObj A$ structure);
2051	    \item $f$ and $g$ agree on a non-empty open subset $U \subseteq C$, i.e.\ $U.\iota \circ f|_{U} = U.\iota \circ g|_{U}$ as scheme morphisms $U \to A$.
2052	  \end{itemize}
2053	  Then $f = g$ as morphisms in $\Over\,(\Spec k)$.
2054	
2055	  In the specialisation $g := c$ the constant morphism at $\eta_A \in A(k)$ (with $\mathrm dc = 0$ automatically because $c$ factors through $\Spec k$, and the agreement on $U$ supplied by promoting the pointing hypothesis $f(p) = \eta_A$ to a chart around $p$ via continuity), this delivers the C.2.d conclusion of \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}: $f$ equals the constant morphism at $\eta_A$.
2056	\end{theorem}
2057	
2058	\begin{proof}
2059	  \uses{lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart, thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
2060	  \leanok
2061	  The proof packages the chart-algebra ($\beta$) chain into the iter-125-refactored \texttt{Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen} idiom. Three steps:
2062	
2063	  \emph{Step 1: reduce $\mathrm df = \mathrm dg$ to a single difference morphism $h$ with $\mathrm dh = 0$.} Using the group structure of $A$, define $h := \mu \circ \langle f, \iota \circ g \rangle \colon C \to A$. The K\"ahler-derivation additivity (Mathlib's \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.D\_sub} / \texttt{Derivation.map\_sub} on the algebra side; lifted to the scheme level via the same functorial chain used in \cref{lem:GrpObj_mulRight_globalises}) gives $\mathrm dh = \mathrm df - \mathrm dg = 0$. The agreement-on-$U$ hypothesis transports to $h|_{U} = \eta_A \circ \pi_C|_{U}$ (i.e.\ on $U$, $h$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$), because $f|_U = g|_U$ implies $\mu(f, \iota \circ g)|_U = \mu(g, \iota \circ g)|_U = \eta_A \circ \pi_C|_U$ by the group identity $b \cdot b^{-1} = \eta_A$ (Mathlib's $\GrpObj$ axiom \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrpObj.mul\_inv\_self} or the equivalent \texttt{lift\_comp\_inv\_*} pattern).
2064	
2065	  \emph{Step 2: apply the per-chart helper \cref{lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart} chart-by-chart on $C$.} For each affine chart $V = \Spec R \subseteq C.\mathrm{left}$ on which the restriction of $h$ lands inside a chart $W = \Spec B$ of $A.\mathrm{left}$ around the image $h(V) \subseteq W$ (such chart pairs exist by quasi-compactness and the standard-smooth presentation of $A.\mathrm{hom}$; chart-uniform existence is automatic from the smoothness of $A.\mathrm{hom}$ on each fibre), the chart-restriction of $h$ is a $k$-algebra map $h^{\sharp} \colon B \to R$ with $\mathrm dB(h^{\sharp}(b)) = 0$ globally (\cref{lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart} consumes the global Step 3 of its proof here). The helper concludes that $h^{\sharp}(b) \in \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,R)$ for every $b \in B$, i.e.\ $h^{\sharp}$ factors as $B \to k \to R$. Assembling chart-by-chart via sheaf compatibility, the scheme-morphism $h \colon C \to A$ factors through $\Spec k \to A$ globally: $h = c' \circ \pi_C$ for a unique constant morphism $c' \colon \Spec k \to A$ determined by the chart-factorisation's image.
2066	
2067	  \emph{Step 3: identify the constant value via Step 1's agreement on $U$.} From Step 1's agreement $h|_{U} = \eta_A \circ \pi_C|_{U}$ and Step 2's factorisation $h = c' \circ \pi_C$, the constant value satisfies $c' \circ \pi_C|_{U} = \eta_A \circ \pi_C|_{U}$, hence $c' = \eta_A$ (the structure map $\pi_C|_{U} \colon U \to \Spec k$ is non-empty since $U$ is non-empty, so the equation on $U$ pins $c'$). Therefore $h = \eta_A \circ \pi_C$ as a morphism $C \to A$, i.e.\ $h$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$. Inverting Step 1's construction $h = \mu \circ \langle f, \iota \circ g \rangle$ gives the cancellation $\mu(f, \iota \circ g) = \eta_A \circ \pi_C \Rightarrow f = g$ via the $\GrpObj$ cancellation identity (Mathlib's \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrpObj.mul\_inv\_eq\_iff} or the equivalent \texttt{lift\_comp\_inv\_*} chain on $\GrpObj A$).
2068	
2069	  Finally, the conclusion is packaged via the iter-125-refactored \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (project lemma \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}, \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Rigidity.lean}): on a non-empty open $U \subseteq C$ where $f|_{U} = g|_{U}$ (supplied by the agreement hypothesis, or by promoting the pointing $f(p) = g(p)$ to a chart around $p$ via continuity), since $C$ is reduced (smooth) and $A$ is separated (proper), the scheme-morphism equality $f = g$ globally follows.
2070	
2071	  Estimated build: $\sim 100$--$150$ LOC. The chart-cover assembly of Step 2 is the dominant cost ($\sim 60$--$90$ LOC); Steps 1 and 3 are $\sim 20$--$30$ LOC each.

2074	\end{proof}

15	# Cotangent space at the identity of a group scheme
16	
17	The cotangent space at the identity of a smooth proper geometrically
18	irreducible group scheme `G` over a base field `k`, defined as a
19	`k`-vector space. Mathematically, this is the pullback of the relative
20	cotangent sheaf `Ω_{G/k}` along the identity section `η_G : 𝟙_ ⟶ G`;
21	the iter-130 Lean realisation (Replacement (B), per
22	`analogies/lieAlgebra-rank-bridge.md`) computes it as the base change to
23	`k` of the algebraic Kähler module `Ω[Γ(G, V) / Γ(Spec k, U)]` on a
24	smooth affine chart `V ⊆ G.left` around the identity-section image.
25	
26	This is piece (i.a) of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile (see
27	`blueprint/src/chapters/RigidityKbar.tex` § "Piece (i): sub-lemma
28	decomposition for iter-128+ build" and STRATEGY.md § M2.body-pile).
29	
30	This file contains the definition `cotangentSpaceAtIdentity` below, the
31	structural-shape acceptance lemma `cotangentSpaceAtIdentity_eq_extendScalars`
32	below, and the companion rank lemma `cotangentSpaceAtIdentity_finrank_eq`
33	below (rank = relative dimension `n` from
34	`[SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom]`, closed iter-132).
35

1	/-
2	Copyright (c) 2022 Andrew Yang. All rights reserved.
3	Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
4	Authors: Andrew Yang
5	-/
6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.TensorProduct.Maps
9	
10	/-!
11	# The characteristic predicate of tensor product
12	
13	## Main definitions
14	
15	- `IsTensorProduct`: A predicate on `f : M₁ →ₗ[R] M₂ →ₗ[R] M` expressing that `f` realizes `M` as
16	  the tensor product of `M₁ ⊗[R] M₂`. This is defined by requiring the lift `M₁ ⊗[R] M₂ → M` to be
17	  bijective.
18	- `IsBaseChange`: A predicate on an `R`-algebra `S` and a map `f : M →ₗ[R] N` with `N` being an
19	  `S`-module, expressing that `f` realizes `N` as the base change of `M` along `R → S`.
20	- `Algebra.IsPushout`: A predicate on the following diagram of scalar towers
21	  ```
22	    R  →  S
23	    ↓     ↓
24	    R' →  S'
25	  ```
26	  asserting that is a pushout diagram (i.e. `S' = S ⊗[R] R'`)
27	
28	## Main results
29	- `TensorProduct.isBaseChange`: `S ⊗[R] M` is the base change of `M` along `R → S`.
30	
31	-/
32	
33	@[expose] public section
34	
35	
36	universe u v₁ v₂ v₃ v₄
37	
38	open TensorProduct
39	
40	section IsTensorProduct
41	
42	variable {R : Type*} [CommSemiring R]
43	variable {M₁ M₂ M M' : Type*}
44	variable [AddCommMonoid M₁] [AddCommMonoid M₂] [AddCommMonoid M] [AddCommMonoid M']
45	variable [Module R M₁] [Module R M₂] [Module R M] [Module R M']
46	variable (f : M₁ →ₗ[R] M₂ →ₗ[R] M)
47	variable {N₁ N₂ N : Type*} [AddCommMonoid N₁] [AddCommMonoid N₂] [AddCommMonoid N]
48	variable [Module R N₁] [Module R N₂] [Module R N]
49	variable {g : N₁ →ₗ[R] N₂ →ₗ[R] N}
50	
51	/-- Given a bilinear map `f : M₁ →ₗ[R] M₂ →ₗ[R] M`, `IsTensorProduct f` means that
52	`M` is the tensor product of `M₁` and `M₂` via `f`.
53	This is defined by requiring the lift `M₁ ⊗[R] M₂ → M` to be bijective.
54	-/
55	def IsTensorProduct : Prop :=
56	  Function.Bijective (TensorProduct.lift f)
57	
58	variable (R M N) {f}
59	
60	theorem TensorProduct.isTensorProduct : IsTensorProduct (TensorProduct.mk R M N) := by
61	  delta IsTensorProduct
62	  convert_to Function.Bijective (LinearMap.id : M ⊗[R] N →ₗ[R] M ⊗[R] N) using 2
63	  · apply TensorProduct.ext'
64	    simp
65	  · exact Function.bijective_id
66	
67	namespace IsTensorProduct
68	
69	variable {R M N}
70	
71	/-- If `M` is the tensor product of `M₁` and `M₂`, it is linearly equivalent to `M₁ ⊗[R] M₂`. -/
72	@[simps! apply]
73	noncomputable def equiv (h : IsTensorProduct f) : M₁ ⊗[R] M₂ ≃ₗ[R] M :=
74	  LinearEquiv.ofBijective _ h
75	
76	@[simp]
77	theorem equiv_toLinearMap (h : IsTensorProduct f) :
78	    h.equiv.toLinearMap = TensorProduct.lift f :=
79	  rfl
80	
81	@[simp]
82	theorem equiv_symm_apply (h : IsTensorProduct f) (x₁ : M₁) (x₂ : M₂) :
83	    h.equiv.symm (f x₁ x₂) = x₁ ⊗ₜ x₂ := by
84	  apply h.equiv.injective
85	  refine (h.equiv.apply_symm_apply _).trans ?_
86	  simp
87	
88	/-- If `M` is the tensor product of `M₁` and `M₂`, we may lift a bilinear map `M₁ →ₗ[R] M₂ →ₗ[R] M'`
89	to a `M →ₗ[R] M'`. -/
90	noncomputable def lift (h : IsTensorProduct f) (f' : M₁ →ₗ[R] M₂ →ₗ[R] M') :
91	    M →ₗ[R] M' :=
92	  (TensorProduct.lift f').comp h.equiv.symm.toLinearMap
93	
94	theorem lift_eq (h : IsTensorProduct f) (f' : M₁ →ₗ[R] M₂ →ₗ[R] M') (x₁ : M₁)
95	    (x₂ : M₂) : h.lift f' (f x₁ x₂) = f' x₁ x₂ := by
96	  simp [lift]
97	
98	/-- The tensor product of a pair of linear maps between modules. -/
99	noncomputable def map (hf : IsTensorProduct f) (hg : IsTensorProduct g)
100	    (i₁ : M₁ →ₗ[R] N₁) (i₂ : M₂ →ₗ[R] N₂) : M →ₗ[R] N :=
101	  hg.equiv.toLinearMap.comp ((TensorProduct.map i₁ i₂).comp hf.equiv.symm.toLinearMap)
102	
103	@[simp]
104	theorem map_eq (hf : IsTensorProduct f) (hg : IsTensorProduct g) (i₁ : M₁ →ₗ[R] N₁)
105	    (i₂ : M₂ →ₗ[R] N₂) (x₁ : M₁) (x₂ : M₂) : hf.map hg i₁ i₂ (f x₁ x₂) = g (i₁ x₁) (i₂ x₂) := by
106	  simp [map]
107	
108	@[elab_as_elim]
109	theorem inductionOn (h : IsTensorProduct f) {motive : M → Prop} (m : M)
110	    (zero : motive 0) (tmul : ∀ x y, motive (f x y))
111	    (add : ∀ x y, motive x → motive y → motive (x + y)) : motive m := by
112	  rw [← h.equiv.right_inv m]
113	  generalize h.equiv.invFun m = y
114	  change motive (TensorProduct.lift f y)
115	  induction y with
116	  | zero => rwa [map_zero]
117	  | tmul _ _ =>
118	    rw [TensorProduct.lift.tmul]
119	    apply tmul
120	  | add _ _ _ _ =>
121	    rw [map_add]
122	    apply add <;> assumption
123	
124	lemma of_equiv (e : M₁ ⊗[R] M₂ ≃ₗ[R] M) (he : ∀ x y, e (x ⊗ₜ y) = f x y) :
125	    IsTensorProduct f := by
126	  have : TensorProduct.lift f = e := by
127	    ext x y
128	    simp [he]
129	  simpa [IsTensorProduct, this] using e.bijective
130	
131	section map
132	
133	variable {P₁ P₂ P : Type*} [AddCommMonoid P₁] [AddCommMonoid P₂]
134	  [AddCommMonoid P] [Module R P₁] [Module R P₂] [Module R P] {p : P₁ →ₗ[R] P₂ →ₗ[R] P}
135	  (hf : IsTensorProduct f) (hg : IsTensorProduct g) (hp : IsTensorProduct p)
136	  (i₁ : N₁ →ₗ[R] P₁) (j₁ : M₁ →ₗ[R] N₁) (i₂ : N₂ →ₗ[R] P₂) (j₂ : M₂ →ₗ[R] N₂)
137	
138	theorem map_comp : hf.map hp (i₁ ∘ₗ j₁) (i₂ ∘ₗ j₂) = hg.map hp i₁ i₂ ∘ₗ hf.map hg j₁ j₂ :=
139	  LinearMap.ext <| fun x ↦ hf.inductionOn x (by simp) (by simp) (fun _ _ h₁ h₂ ↦ by simp [h₁, h₂])
140	
141	theorem map_map (x : M) :
142	    hg.map hp i₁ i₂ ((hf.map hg j₁ j₂) x) = hf.map hp (i₁ ∘ₗ j₁) (i₂ ∘ₗ j₂) x :=
143	  DFunLike.congr_fun (hf.map_comp hg hp i₁ j₁ i₂ j₂).symm x
144	
145	@[simp]
146	theorem map_id :
147	    hf.map hf (LinearMap.id : M₁ →ₗ[R] M₁) (LinearMap.id : M₂ →ₗ[R] M₂) = LinearMap.id :=
148	  LinearMap.ext <| fun x ↦ hf.inductionOn x (by simp) (by simp) (fun _ _ h₁ h₂ ↦ by simp [h₁, h₂])
149	
150	@[simp]
151	protected theorem map_one : hf.map hf (1 : M₁ →ₗ[R] M₁) (1 : M₂ →ₗ[R] M₂) = 1 :=
152	  hf.map_id
153	
154	protected theorem map_mul (i₁ i₂ : M₁ →ₗ[R] M₁) (j₁ j₂ : M₂ →ₗ[R] M₂) :
155	    hf.map hf (i₁ * i₂) (j₁ * j₂) = hf.map hf i₁ j₁ * hf.map hf i₂ j₂ :=
156	  hf.map_comp hf hf i₁ i₂ j₁ j₂
157	
158	protected theorem map_pow (i : M₁ →ₗ[R] M₁) (j : M₂ →ₗ[R] M₂) (n : ℕ) :
159	    hf.map hf i j ^ n = hf.map hf (i ^ n) (j ^ n) := by
160	  induction n with
161	  | zero => simp
162	  | succ n ih => simp only [pow_succ, ih, hf.map_mul]
163	
164	end map
165	
166	
167	section
168	
169	variable {R S : Type*} [CommSemiring R] [CommSemiring S] [Algebra R S]
170	 {M₁ M₂ M₃ M₁₂ M₂₃ : Type*} [AddCommMonoid M₁] [AddCommMonoid M₂] [AddCommMonoid M₃]
171	 [AddCommMonoid M₁₂] [AddCommMonoid M₂₃]
172	 [Module R M₁]
173	 [Module R M₂] [Module S M₂] [IsScalarTower R S M₂]
174	 [Module R M₃] [Module S M₃] [IsScalarTower R S M₃]
175	 [Module R M₁₂] [Module S M₁₂] [IsScalarTower R S M₁₂]
176	 [Module R M₂₃] [Module S M₂₃] [IsScalarTower R S M₂₃]
177	
178	/-- (Implementation): Use the more linear `IsTensorProduct.assoc`. -/
179	private noncomputable def assocAux
180	    (f : M₁ →ₗ[R] M₂ →ₗ[S] M₁₂) (hf : IsTensorProduct (f.restrictScalars₁₂ R R))
181	    (g : M₂ →ₗ[S] M₃ →ₗ[S] M₂₃) (hg : IsTensorProduct g) :
182	    M₁₂ ⊗[S] M₃ ≃ₗ[R] M₁ ⊗[R] M₂₃ :=
183	  letI : Module S (M₁ ⊗[R] M₂) :=
184	    AddEquiv.module S hf.equiv.toAddEquiv
185	  haveI heq (s : S) (y : M₁) (x : M₂) : s • y ⊗ₜ[R] x = y ⊗ₜ[R] (s • x) := by
186	    change hf.equiv.symm (s • _) = _
187	    dsimp
188	    rw [← map_smul]
189	    apply hf.equiv_symm_apply
190	  haveI : IsScalarTower R S (M₁ ⊗[R] M₂) := hf.equiv.isScalarTower S
191	  letI e₀ : M₂ ⊗[R] M₁ ≃ₗ[S] M₁ ⊗[R] M₂ :=
192	    { __ := TensorProduct.comm R M₂ M₁
193	      map_smul' s x := by induction x <;> simp_all [TensorProduct.smul_tmul'] }
194	  LinearEquiv.symm <|
195	    TensorProduct.congr (.refl _ _) (hg.equiv.symm.restrictScalars R) ≪≫ₗ
196	    TensorProduct.comm _ _ _ ≪≫ₗ
197	    (AlgebraTensorModule.congr (TensorProduct.comm _ _ _) (.refl _ _)).restrictScalars R ≪≫ₗ
198	    (AlgebraTensorModule.assoc R S S M₃ M₂ M₁).restrictScalars R ≪≫ₗ
199	    (TensorProduct.comm _ _ _).restrictScalars R ≪≫ₗ
200	    (TensorProduct.congr e₀ (.refl _ _)).restrictScalars R ≪≫ₗ
201	    (TensorProduct.congr (hf.equiv.linearEquiv S) (.refl _ _)).restrictScalars R
202	
203	variable (f : M₁ →ₗ[R] M₂ →ₗ[S] M₁₂) (hf : IsTensorProduct (f.restrictScalars₁₂ R R))
204	  (g : M₂ →ₗ[S] M₃ →ₗ[S] M₂₃) (hg : IsTensorProduct g)
205	
206	@[simp]
207	private lemma assocAux_symm_tmul (x₁ : M₁) (x₂ : M₂) (x₃ : M₃) :
208	    (IsTensorProduct.assocAux f hf g hg).symm (x₁ ⊗ₜ g x₂ x₃) = f x₁ x₂ ⊗ₜ x₃ := by
209	  simp [IsTensorProduct.assocAux]
210	
211	@[simp]
212	private lemma assocAux_tmul (x₁ : M₁) (x₂ : M₂) (x₃ : M₃) :
213	    IsTensorProduct.assocAux f hf g hg (f x₁ x₂ ⊗ₜ x₃) = x₁ ⊗ₜ g x₂ x₃ := by
214	  have : hf.equiv.symm (f x₁ x₂) = x₁ ⊗ₜ x₂ := hf.equiv_symm_apply _ _
215	  simp [IsTensorProduct.assocAux, this]
216	
217	/--
218	This is the canonical isomorphism `(M₁ ⊗[R] M₂) ⊗[S] M₃ ≃ₗ[T] M₁ ⊗[R] (M₂ ⊗[S] M₃)`.
219	We state this for a general `M₁₂ = M₁ ⊗[R] M₂` and `M₂₃ = M₂ ⊗[R] M₃`.
220	For the version where `R` and `S` are flipped, see `TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.assoc`.
221	-/
222	@[no_expose]
223	noncomputable def assoc {T : Type*} [CommSemiring T] [Algebra R T] [Module T M₁]
224	    [IsScalarTower R T M₁] [Module T M₁₂] [SMulCommClass S T M₁₂] [IsScalarTower R T M₁₂]
225	    (f : M₁ →ₗ[T] M₂ →ₗ[S] M₁₂) (hf : IsTensorProduct (f.restrictScalars₁₂ R R))
226	    (g : M₂ →ₗ[S] M₃ →ₗ[S] M₂₃) (hg : IsTensorProduct g) :
227	    M₁₂ ⊗[S] M₃ ≃ₗ[T] M₁ ⊗[R] M₂₃ where
228	  toAddEquiv := IsTensorProduct.assocAux (f.restrictScalars₁₂ R S) hf g hg
229	  map_smul' t x := by
230	    induction x with
231	    | zero => simp
232	    | add x y _ _ => simp_all
233	    | tmul x y =>
234	    obtain ⟨x, rfl⟩ := hf.equiv.surjective x
235	    induction x with
236	    | zero => simp
237	    | add x y _ _ => simp_all [add_tmul]
238	    | tmul x z =>
239	      have : t • (f x) z = f (t • x) z := by simp
240	      dsimp
241	      rw [smul_tmul', this, ← f.restrictScalars₁₂_apply_apply R S,
242	        ← f.restrictScalars₁₂_apply_apply R S, IsTensorProduct.assocAux_tmul,
243	        IsTensorProduct.assocAux_tmul, TensorProduct.smul_tmul']
244	
245	variable {T : Type*} [CommSemiring T] [Algebra R T] [Module T M₁] [IsScalarTower R T M₁]
246	  [Module T M₁₂] [SMulCommClass S T M₁₂] [IsScalarTower R T M₁₂]
247	  (f : M₁ →ₗ[T] M₂ →ₗ[S] M₁₂) (hf : IsTensorProduct (f.restrictScalars₁₂ R R))
248	  (g : M₂ →ₗ[S] M₃ →ₗ[S] M₂₃) (hg : IsTensorProduct g)
249	
250	@[simp]
251	lemma assoc_tmul (x₁ : M₁) (x₂ : M₂) (x₃ : M₃) :
252	    assoc f hf g hg (f x₁ x₂ ⊗ₜ x₃) = x₁ ⊗ₜ g x₂ x₃ :=
253	  assocAux_tmul (f.restrictScalars₁₂ R S) hf g hg _ _ _
254	
255	@[simp]
256	lemma assoc_symm_tmul (x₁ : M₁) (x₂ : M₂) (x₃ : M₃) :
257	    (assoc f hf g hg).symm (x₁ ⊗ₜ g x₂ x₃) = f x₁ x₂ ⊗ₜ x₃ :=
258	  assocAux_symm_tmul (f.restrictScalars₁₂ R S) hf g hg _ _ _
259	
260	/-- Variant of `IsTensorProduct.assoc` taking an `R`-bilinear map `f` and proofs that
261	`f` is `T` linear in the first and `S`-linear in the second argument. -/
262	noncomputable def assocOfMapSMul (f : M₁ →ₗ[R] M₂ →ₗ[R] M₁₂) (hf : IsTensorProduct f)
263	    (g : M₂ →ₗ[S] M₃ →ₗ[S] M₂₃) (hg : IsTensorProduct g)
264	    (h₁ : ∀ (t : T) (x : M₁) (y : M₂), f (t • x) y = t • f x y)
265	    (h₂ : ∀ (s : S) (x : M₁) (y : M₂), f x (s • y) = s • f x y) :
266	    M₁₂ ⊗[S] M₃ ≃ₗ[T] M₁ ⊗[R] M₂₃ :=
267	  IsTensorProduct.assoc (.mk₂' _ _ (f ·) (by simp) (by simp [h₁]) (by simp) (by simp [h₂])) hf g hg
268	
269	variable (f : M₁ →ₗ[R] M₂ →ₗ[R] M₁₂) (hf : IsTensorProduct f)
270	  (g : M₂ →ₗ[S] M₃ →ₗ[S] M₂₃) (hg : IsTensorProduct g)
271	  (h₁ : ∀ (t : T) (x : M₁) (y : M₂), f (t • x) y = t • f x y)
272	  (h₂ : ∀ (s : S) (x : M₁) (y : M₂), f x (s • y) = s • f x y)
273	
274	@[simp]
275	lemma assocOfMapSMul_tmul (x₁ : M₁) (x₂ : M₂) (x₃ : M₃) :
276	    assocOfMapSMul f hf g hg h₁ h₂ (f x₁ x₂ ⊗ₜ x₃) = x₁ ⊗ₜ g x₂ x₃ :=
277	  IsTensorProduct.assoc_tmul ..
278	
279	@[simp]
280	lemma assocOfMapSMul_symm_tmul (x₁ : M₁) (x₂ : M₂) (x₃ : M₃) :
281	    (assocOfMapSMul f hf g hg h₁ h₂).symm (x₁ ⊗ₜ g x₂ x₃) = f x₁ x₂ ⊗ₜ x₃ :=
282	  IsTensorProduct.assoc_symm_tmul ..
283	
284	end
285	
286	end IsTensorProduct
287	
288	end IsTensorProduct

290	section IsBaseChange
291	
292	variable {R : Type*} {M : Type v₁} {N : Type v₂} (S : Type v₃)
293	variable [AddCommMonoid M] [AddCommMonoid N] [CommSemiring R]
294	variable [CommSemiring S] [Algebra R S] [Module R M] [Module R N] [Module S N] [IsScalarTower R S N]
295	variable (f : M →ₗ[R] N)
296	
297	/-- Given an `R`-algebra `S` and an `R`-module `M`, an `S`-module `N` together with a map
298	`f : M →ₗ[R] N` is the base change of `M` to `S` if the map `S × M → N, (s, m) ↦ s • f m` is the
299	tensor product. -/
300	def IsBaseChange : Prop :=
301	  IsTensorProduct
302	    (((Algebra.linearMap S <| Module.End S (M →ₗ[R] N)).flip f).restrictScalars R)
303	
304	variable {S f} (h : IsBaseChange S f)
305	variable {P Q : Type*} [AddCommMonoid P] [Module R P] [AddCommMonoid Q] [Module S Q]
306	
307	section
308	
309	variable [Module R Q] [IsScalarTower R S Q]
310	
311	/-- Suppose `f : M →ₗ[R] N` is the base change of `M` along `R → S`. Then any `R`-linear map from
312	`M` to an `S`-module factors through `f`. -/
313	noncomputable nonrec def IsBaseChange.lift (g : M →ₗ[R] Q) : N →ₗ[S] Q :=
314	  { h.lift
315	      (((Algebra.linearMap S <| Module.End S (M →ₗ[R] Q)).flip g).restrictScalars R) with
316	    map_smul' := fun r x => by
317	      let F := ((Algebra.linearMap S <| Module.End S (M →ₗ[R] Q)).flip g).restrictScalars R
318	      have hF : ∀ (s : S) (m : M), h.lift F (s • f m) = s • g m := h.lift_eq F
319	      change h.lift F (r • x) = r • h.lift F x
320	      induction x using h.inductionOn with
321	      | zero => rw [smul_zero, map_zero, smul_zero]
322	      | tmul s m =>
323	        change h.lift F (r • s • f m) = r • h.lift F (s • f m)
324	        rw [← mul_smul, hF, hF, mul_smul]
325	      | add x₁ x₂ e₁ e₂ => rw [map_add, smul_add, map_add, smul_add, e₁, e₂] }
326	
327	nonrec theorem IsBaseChange.lift_eq (g : M →ₗ[R] Q) (x : M) : h.lift g (f x) = g x := by
328	  have hF : ∀ (s : S) (m : M), h.lift g (s • f m) = s • g m := h.lift_eq _
329	  convert hF 1 x <;> rw [one_smul]
330	
331	theorem IsBaseChange.lift_comp (g : M →ₗ[R] Q) : ((h.lift g).restrictScalars R).comp f = g :=
332	  LinearMap.ext (h.lift_eq g)
333	
334	end
335	
336	section
337	include h
338	
339	@[elab_as_elim]
340	nonrec theorem IsBaseChange.inductionOn (x : N) (motive : N → Prop) (zero : motive 0)
341	    (tmul : ∀ m : M, motive (f m)) (smul : ∀ (s : S) (n), motive n → motive (s • n))
342	    (add : ∀ n₁ n₂, motive n₁ → motive n₂ → motive (n₁ + n₂)) : motive x :=
343	  h.inductionOn x zero (fun _ _ => smul _ _ (tmul _)) add
344	
345	theorem IsBaseChange.algHom_ext (g₁ g₂ : N →ₗ[S] Q) (e : ∀ x, g₁ (f x) = g₂ (f x)) : g₁ = g₂ := by
346	  ext x
347	  refine h.inductionOn x _ ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_
348	  · rw [map_zero, map_zero]
349	  · assumption
350	  · intro s n e'
351	    rw [g₁.map_smul, g₂.map_smul, e']
352	  · intro x y e₁ e₂
353	    rw [map_add, map_add, e₁, e₂]
354	
355	theorem IsBaseChange.algHom_ext' [Module R Q] [IsScalarTower R S Q] (g₁ g₂ : N →ₗ[S] Q)
356	    (e : (g₁.restrictScalars R).comp f = (g₂.restrictScalars R).comp f) : g₁ = g₂ :=
357	  h.algHom_ext g₁ g₂ (LinearMap.congr_fun e)
358	
359	end
360	
361	variable (R M N S)
362	
363	theorem TensorProduct.isBaseChange : IsBaseChange S (TensorProduct.mk R S M 1) := by
364	  delta IsBaseChange
365	  convert TensorProduct.isTensorProduct R S M using 1
366	  ext s x
367	  change s • (1 : S) ⊗ₜ[R] x = s ⊗ₜ[R] x
368	  rw [TensorProduct.smul_tmul']
369	  congr 1
370	  exact mul_one _
371	
372	variable {R M N S}
373	
374	/-- The base change of `M` along `R → S` is linearly equivalent to `S ⊗[R] M`. -/
375	noncomputable nonrec def IsBaseChange.equiv : S ⊗[R] M ≃ₗ[S] N :=
376	  { h.equiv with
377	    map_smul' := fun r x => by
378	      change h.equiv (r • x) = r • h.equiv x
379	      refine TensorProduct.induction_on x ?_ ?_ ?_
380	      · rw [smul_zero, map_zero, smul_zero]
381	      · intro x y
382	        simp [smul_tmul', Algebra.linearMap_apply, smul_comm r x]
383	      · intro x y hx hy
384	        rw [map_add, smul_add, map_add, smul_add, hx, hy] }
385	
386	@[simp]
387	theorem IsBaseChange.equiv_tmul (s : S) (m : M) : h.equiv (s ⊗ₜ m) = s • f m :=
388	  rfl
389	
390	@[simp]
391	theorem IsBaseChange.equiv_symm_apply (m : M) : h.equiv.symm (f m) = 1 ⊗ₜ m := by
392	  rw [h.equiv.symm_apply_eq, h.equiv_tmul, one_smul]
393	
394	lemma IsBaseChange.of_equiv (e : S ⊗[R] M ≃ₗ[S] N) (he : ∀ x, e (1 ⊗ₜ x) = f x) :
395	    IsBaseChange S f := by
396	  apply IsTensorProduct.of_equiv (e.restrictScalars R)
397	  intro x y
398	  simp [show x ⊗ₜ[R] y = x • (1 ⊗ₜ[R] y) by simp [smul_tmul'], he]
399	
400	variable (R S) in
401	theorem IsBaseChange.linearMap : IsBaseChange S (Algebra.linearMap R S) :=
402	  of_equiv (AlgebraTensorModule.rid R S S) fun x ↦ by
403	    simpa using (Algebra.algebraMap_eq_smul_one x).symm
404	
405	section
406	
407	variable (A : Type*) [CommSemiring A]
408	variable [Algebra R A] [Algebra S A] [IsScalarTower R S A]
409	variable [Module S M] [IsScalarTower R S M]
410	variable [Module A N] [IsScalarTower S A N] [IsScalarTower R A N]
411	
412	/-- If `N` is the base change of `M` to `A`, then `N ⊗[R] P` is the base change
413	of `M ⊗[R] P` to `A`. This is simply the isomorphism
414	`A ⊗[S] (M ⊗[R] P) ≃ₗ[A] (A ⊗[S] M) ⊗[R] P`. -/
415	lemma isBaseChange_tensorProduct_map {f : M →ₗ[S] N} (hf : IsBaseChange A f) :
416	    IsBaseChange A (AlgebraTensorModule.map f (LinearMap.id (R := R) (M := P))) := by
417	  let e : A ⊗[S] (M ⊗[R] P) ≃ₗ[A] N ⊗[R] P := (AlgebraTensorModule.assoc R S A A M P).symm.trans
418	    (AlgebraTensorModule.congr hf.equiv (LinearEquiv.refl R P))
419	  refine IsBaseChange.of_equiv e (fun x ↦ ?_)
420	  induction x with
421	  | zero => simp
422	  | tmul => simp [e, IsBaseChange.equiv_tmul]
423	  | add _ _ h1 h2 => simp [tmul_add, h1, h2]
424	
425	end
426	
427	variable (f) in
428	theorem IsBaseChange.of_lift_unique
429	    (h : ∀ (Q : Type max v₁ v₂ v₃) [AddCommMonoid Q],
430	      ∀ [Module R Q] [Module S Q], ∀ [IsScalarTower R S Q],
431	        ∀ g : M →ₗ[R] Q, ∃! g' : N →ₗ[S] Q, (g'.restrictScalars R).comp f = g) :
432	    IsBaseChange S f := by
433	  obtain ⟨g, hg, -⟩ :=
434	    h (ULift.{v₂} <| S ⊗[R] M)
435	      (ULift.moduleEquiv.symm.toLinearMap.comp <| TensorProduct.mk R S M 1)
436	  let f' : S ⊗[R] M →ₗ[R] N :=
437	    TensorProduct.lift (((LinearMap.flip (AlgHom.toLinearMap (Algebra.ofId S
438	      (Module.End S (M →ₗ[R] N))))) f).restrictScalars R)
439	  change Function.Bijective f'
440	  let f'' : S ⊗[R] M →ₗ[S] N := by
441	    refine
442	      { f' with
443	        map_smul' := fun s x =>
444	          TensorProduct.induction_on x ?_ (fun s' y => smul_assoc s s' _) fun x y hx hy => ?_ }
445	    · dsimp; rw [map_zero, smul_zero, map_zero, smul_zero]
446	    · dsimp at *; rw [smul_add, map_add, map_add, smul_add, hx, hy]
447	  simp_rw [DFunLike.ext_iff, LinearMap.comp_apply, LinearMap.restrictScalars_apply] at hg
448	  let fe : S ⊗[R] M ≃ₗ[S] N :=
449	    LinearEquiv.ofLinear f'' (ULift.moduleEquiv.toLinearMap.comp g) ?_ ?_
450	  · exact fe.bijective
451	  · rw [← LinearMap.cancel_left (ULift.moduleEquiv : ULift.{max v₁ v₃} N ≃ₗ[S] N).symm.injective]
452	    refine (h (ULift.{max v₁ v₃} N) <| ULift.moduleEquiv.symm.toLinearMap.comp f).unique ?_ rfl
453	    ext x
454	    simp only [LinearMap.comp_apply, LinearMap.restrictScalars_apply, hg]
455	    apply one_smul
456	  · ext x
457	    change (g <| (1 : S) • f x).down = _
458	    rw [one_smul, hg]
459	    rfl
460	
461	theorem IsBaseChange.iff_lift_unique :
462	    IsBaseChange S f ↔
463	      ∀ (Q : Type max v₁ v₂ v₃) [AddCommMonoid Q],
464	        ∀ [Module R Q] [Module S Q],
465	          ∀ [IsScalarTower R S Q],
466	            ∀ g : M →ₗ[R] Q, ∃! g' : N →ₗ[S] Q, (g'.restrictScalars R).comp f = g :=
467	  ⟨fun h => by
468	    intro Q _ _ _ _ g
469	    exact ⟨h.lift g, h.lift_comp g, fun g' e => h.algHom_ext' _ _ (e.trans (h.lift_comp g).symm)⟩,
470	    IsBaseChange.of_lift_unique f⟩
471	
472	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
473	theorem IsBaseChange.ofEquiv (e : M ≃ₗ[R] N) : IsBaseChange R e.toLinearMap := by
474	  apply IsBaseChange.of_lift_unique
475	  intro Q I₁ I₂ I₃ I₄ g
476	  have : I₂ = I₃ := by
477	    ext r q
478	    change (by let _ := I₂; exact r • q) = (by let _ := I₃; exact r • q)
479	    dsimp
480	    rw [← one_smul R q, smul_smul, ← @smul_assoc _ _ _ (id _) (id _) (id _) I₄, smul_eq_mul]
481	  cases this
482	  refine
483	    ⟨g.comp e.symm.toLinearMap, by
484	      ext
485	      simp, ?_⟩
486	  rintro y (rfl : _ = _)
487	  ext
488	  simp
489	
490	variable {T O : Type*} [CommSemiring T] [Algebra R T] [Algebra S T] [IsScalarTower R S T]
491	variable [AddCommMonoid O] [Module R O] [Module S O] [Module T O] [IsScalarTower S T O]
492	variable [IsScalarTower R S O] [IsScalarTower R T O]
493	
494	theorem IsBaseChange.comp {f : M →ₗ[R] N} (hf : IsBaseChange S f) {g : N →ₗ[S] O}
495	    (hg : IsBaseChange T g) : IsBaseChange T ((g.restrictScalars R).comp f) := by
496	  apply IsBaseChange.of_lift_unique
497	  intro Q _ _ _ _ i
498	  letI := Module.compHom Q (algebraMap S T)
499	  haveI : IsScalarTower S T Q :=
500	    ⟨fun x y z => by
501	      rw [Algebra.smul_def, mul_smul]
502	      rfl⟩
503	  have : IsScalarTower R S Q := IsScalarTower.to₁₂₄ _ _ T _
504	  refine
505	    ⟨hg.lift (hf.lift i), by
506	      ext
507	      simp [IsBaseChange.lift_eq], ?_⟩
508	  rintro g' (e : _ = _)
509	  refine hg.algHom_ext' _ _ (hf.algHom_ext' _ _ ?_)
510	  rw [IsBaseChange.lift_comp, IsBaseChange.lift_comp, ← e]
511	  ext
512	  rfl
513	
514	/-- If `N` is the base change of `M` to `S` and `O` the base change of `M` to `T`, then
515	`O` is the base change of `N` to `T`. -/
516	lemma IsBaseChange.of_comp {f : M →ₗ[R] N} (hf : IsBaseChange S f) {h : N →ₗ[S] O}
517	    (hc : IsBaseChange T ((h : N →ₗ[R] O) ∘ₗ f)) :
518	    IsBaseChange T h := by
519	  apply IsBaseChange.of_lift_unique
520	  intro Q _ _ _ _ r
521	  letI : Module R Q := .restrictScalars R S Q
522	  haveI : IsScalarTower R S Q := .restrictScalars R S Q
523	  haveI : IsScalarTower R T Q := IsScalarTower.of_algebraMap_smul fun r x ↦ by
524	    simp [IsScalarTower.algebraMap_apply R S T]
525	  let r' : M →ₗ[R] Q := r ∘ₗ f
526	  let q : O →ₗ[T] Q := hc.lift r'
527	  refine ⟨q, ?_, ?_⟩
528	  · apply hf.algHom_ext'
529	    simp [r', q, LinearMap.comp_assoc, hc.lift_comp]
530	  · intro q' hq'
531	    apply hc.algHom_ext'
532	    apply_fun LinearMap.restrictScalars R at hq'
533	    rw [← LinearMap.comp_assoc]
534	    rw [show q'.restrictScalars R ∘ₗ h.restrictScalars R = _ from hq', hc.lift_comp]
535	
536	/-- If `N` is the base change `M` to `S`, then `O` is the base change of `M` to `T` if and
537	only if `O` is the base change of `N` to `T`. -/
538	lemma IsBaseChange.comp_iff {f : M →ₗ[R] N} (hf : IsBaseChange S f) {h : N →ₗ[S] O} :
539	    IsBaseChange T ((h : N →ₗ[R] O) ∘ₗ f) ↔ IsBaseChange T h :=
540	  ⟨fun hc ↦ IsBaseChange.of_comp hf hc, fun hh ↦ IsBaseChange.comp hf hh⟩
541	
542	/-- Let `R` be a commutative ring, `S` be an `R`-algebra, `M` be an `R`-module, `P` be an `S`
543	  module, `N` be the base change of `M` to `S`, then `P ⊗[S] N` is isomorphic to `P ⊗[R] M`
544	  as `S`-modules. -/
545	noncomputable def IsBaseChange.tensorEquiv {f : M →ₗ[R] N} (hf : IsBaseChange S f) (P : Type*)
546	    [AddCommGroup P] [Module R P] [Module S P] [IsScalarTower R S P] : P ⊗[S] N ≃ₗ[S] P ⊗[R] M :=
547	  LinearEquiv.lTensor P hf.equiv.symm ≪≫ₗ AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange R S S P M
548	
549	theorem IsBaseChange.map_id_lsmul_eq_lsmul_algebraMap
550	    {f : M →ₗ[R] N} (hf : IsBaseChange S f) (x : R) :
551	    hf.map hf LinearMap.id (LinearMap.lsmul R M x) = LinearMap.lsmul S N (algebraMap R S x) := by
552	  ext y
553	  refine IsTensorProduct.inductionOn hf y (by simp) ?_ (fun _ _ ha hb ↦ by simp [ha, hb])
554	  intro s m
555	  rw [hf.map_eq hf]
556	  simpa using smul_comm x s (f m)
557	
558	variable {R' S' : Type*} [CommSemiring R'] [CommSemiring S']
559	variable [Algebra R R'] [Algebra S S'] [Algebra R' S'] [Algebra R S']
560	variable [IsScalarTower R R' S'] [IsScalarTower R S S']
561	
562	open IsScalarTower (toAlgHom algebraMap_apply)
563	
564	variable (R S R' S')
565	
566	/-- A type-class stating that the following diagram of scalar towers
567	```
568	R  →  S
569	↓     ↓
570	R' →  S'
571	```
572	is a pushout diagram (i.e. `S' = S ⊗[R] R'`)
573	-/
574	@[mk_iff]
575	class Algebra.IsPushout : Prop where
576	  out : IsBaseChange S (toAlgHom R R' S').toLinearMap
577	
578	/-- The isomorphism `S' ≃ S ⊗[R] R` given `Algebra.IsPushout R S R' S'`. -/
579	noncomputable
580	def Algebra.IsPushout.equiv [h : Algebra.IsPushout R S R' S'] : S ⊗[R] R' ≃ₐ[S] S' where
581	  __ := h.out.equiv
582	  map_mul' x y := by
583	    dsimp
584	    induction x with
585	    | zero => simp
586	    | add x y _ _ => simp [*, add_mul]
587	    | tmul a b =>
588	      induction y with
589	      | zero => simp
590	      | add x y _ _ => simp [*, mul_add]
591	      | tmul x y => simp [IsBaseChange.equiv_tmul, Algebra.smul_def, mul_mul_mul_comm]
592	  commutes' := by simp [IsBaseChange.equiv_tmul, Algebra.smul_def]
593	
594	lemma Algebra.IsPushout.equiv_tmul [h : Algebra.IsPushout R S R' S'] (a : S) (b : R') :
595	    equiv R S R' S' (a ⊗ₜ b) = algebraMap _ _ a * algebraMap _ _ b :=
596	  (h.out.equiv_tmul _ _).trans (Algebra.smul_def _ _)
597	
598	lemma Algebra.IsPushout.equiv_symm_algebraMap_left [Algebra.IsPushout R S R' S'] (a : S) :
599	    (equiv R S R' S').symm (algebraMap S S' a) = a ⊗ₜ 1 := by
600	  rw [(equiv R S R' S').symm_apply_eq, equiv_tmul, map_one, mul_one]
601	
602	lemma Algebra.IsPushout.equiv_symm_algebraMap_right [Algebra.IsPushout R S R' S'] (a : R') :
603	    (equiv R S R' S').symm (algebraMap R' S' a) = 1 ⊗ₜ a := by
604	  rw [(equiv R S R' S').symm_apply_eq, equiv_tmul, map_one, one_mul]
605	
606	variable {R S R' S'}
607	
608	@[symm]
609	theorem Algebra.IsPushout.symm (h : Algebra.IsPushout R S R' S') : Algebra.IsPushout R R' S S' where
610	  out := .of_equiv
611	    { __ := (TensorProduct.comm R ..).toAddEquiv.trans (equiv R S R' S').toAddEquiv,
612	      map_smul' _ x := x.induction_on (by simp) (fun _ _ ↦ by
613	        simp [equiv_tmul, Algebra.smul_def, mul_left_comm]) (by simp +contextual) }
614	    fun _ ↦ by simp [equiv_tmul]
615	
616	variable (R S R' S')
617	
618	theorem Algebra.IsPushout.comm : Algebra.IsPushout R S R' S' ↔ Algebra.IsPushout R R' S S' :=
619	  ⟨Algebra.IsPushout.symm, Algebra.IsPushout.symm⟩
620	
621	instance : Algebra.IsPushout R R S S where
622	  out := .of_equiv (TensorProduct.lid R S) fun _ ↦ by simp
623	
624	instance : Algebra.IsPushout R S R S := .symm inferInstance

627	
628	attribute [local instance] Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra
629	
630	instance TensorProduct.isPushout {R S T : Type*} [CommSemiring R] [CommSemiring S] [CommSemiring T]
631	    [Algebra R S] [Algebra R T] : Algebra.IsPushout R S T (S ⊗[R] T) :=
632	  ⟨TensorProduct.isBaseChange R T S⟩
633	
634	instance TensorProduct.isPushout' {R S T : Type*} [CommSemiring R] [CommSemiring S] [CommSemiring T]
635	    [Algebra R S] [Algebra R T] : Algebra.IsPushout R T S (S ⊗[R] T) :=
636	  Algebra.IsPushout.symm inferInstance

638	lemma Algebra.IsPushout.tensorProduct_tensorProduct
639	    (R S A B : Type*) [CommSemiring R] [CommSemiring S] [CommSemiring A] [CommSemiring B]
640	    [Algebra R A] [Algebra R B] [Algebra A B] [IsScalarTower R A B] [Algebra R S]
641	    {_ : Algebra (A ⊗[R] S) (B ⊗[R] S)} {_ : IsScalarTower A (A ⊗[R] S) (B ⊗[R] S)}
642	    (H : (algebraMap (A ⊗[R] S) (B ⊗[R] S)).comp Algebra.TensorProduct.includeRight.toRingHom =
643	      Algebra.TensorProduct.includeRight.toRingHom) :
644	    Algebra.IsPushout A B (A ⊗[R] S) (B ⊗[R] S) := by
645	  constructor
646	  convert isBaseChange_tensorProduct_map (R := R) (P := S) _ (IsBaseChange.linearMap A B)
647	  ext s
648	  simpa using congr($H s)
649	
650	/-- If `S' = S ⊗[R] R'`, then any pair of `R`-algebra homomorphisms `f : S → A` and `g : R' → A`
651	such that `f x` and `g y` commutes for all `x, y` descends to a (unique) homomorphism `S' → A`.
652	-/
653	@[simps! -isSimp apply]
654	noncomputable def Algebra.pushoutDesc [H : Algebra.IsPushout R S R' S'] {A : Type*} [Semiring A]
655	    [Algebra R A] (f : S →ₐ[R] A) (g : R' →ₐ[R] A) (hf : ∀ x y, f x * g y = g y * f x) :
656	    S' →ₐ[R] A :=
657	  (Algebra.TensorProduct.lift f g hf).comp
658	    ((Algebra.IsPushout.equiv R S R' S').symm.toAlgHom.restrictScalars R)
659	
660	@[simp]
661	theorem Algebra.pushoutDesc_left [Algebra.IsPushout R S R' S'] {A : Type*} [Semiring A]
662	    [Algebra R A] (f : S →ₐ[R] A) (g : R' →ₐ[R] A) (H) (x : S) :
663	    Algebra.pushoutDesc S' f g H (algebraMap S S' x) = f x := by
664	  simp [Algebra.pushoutDesc_apply]
665	
666	theorem Algebra.lift_algHom_comp_left [Algebra.IsPushout R S R' S'] {A : Type*} [Semiring A]
667	    [Algebra R A] (f : S →ₐ[R] A) (g : R' →ₐ[R] A) (H) :
668	    (Algebra.pushoutDesc S' f g H).comp (toAlgHom R S S') = f :=
669	  AlgHom.ext fun x => (Algebra.pushoutDesc_left S' f g H x :)
670	
671	@[simp]
672	theorem Algebra.pushoutDesc_right [Algebra.IsPushout R S R' S'] {A : Type*} [Semiring A]
673	    [Algebra R A] (f : S →ₐ[R] A) (g : R' →ₐ[R] A) (H) (x : R') :
674	    Algebra.pushoutDesc S' f g H (algebraMap R' S' x) = g x := by
675	  simp [Algebra.pushoutDesc_apply, Algebra.IsPushout.equiv_symm_algebraMap_right]
676	
677	theorem Algebra.lift_algHom_comp_right [Algebra.IsPushout R S R' S'] {A : Type*} [Semiring A]
678	    [Algebra R A] (f : S →ₐ[R] A) (g : R' →ₐ[R] A) (H) :
679	    (Algebra.pushoutDesc S' f g H).comp (toAlgHom R R' S') = g :=
680	  AlgHom.ext fun x => (Algebra.pushoutDesc_right S' f g H x :)
681	
682	@[ext (iff := false)]
683	theorem Algebra.IsPushout.algHom_ext [H : Algebra.IsPushout R S R' S'] {A : Type*} [Semiring A]
684	    [Algebra R A] {f g : S' →ₐ[R] A} (h₁ : f.comp (toAlgHom R R' S') = g.comp (toAlgHom R R' S'))
685	    (h₂ : f.comp (toAlgHom R S S') = g.comp (toAlgHom R S S')) : f = g := by
686	  ext x
687	  refine H.1.inductionOn x _ ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_
688	  · simp only [map_zero]
689	  · exact AlgHom.congr_fun h₁
690	  · intro s s' e
691	    rw [Algebra.smul_def, map_mul, map_mul, e]
692	    congr 1
693	    exact (AlgHom.congr_fun h₂ s :)
694	  · intro s₁ s₂ e₁ e₂
695	    rw [map_add, map_add, e₁, e₂]

697	variable (R S R')
698	/--
699	Let the following be a commutative diagram of rings
700	```
701	  R  →  S  →  T
702	  ↓     ↓     ↓
703	  R' →  S' →  T'
704	```
705	where the left-hand square is a pushout. Then the following are equivalent:
706	- the big rectangle is a pushout.
707	- the right-hand square is a pushout.
708	
709	Note that this is essentially the isomorphism `T ⊗[S] (S ⊗[R] R') ≃ₐ[T] T ⊗[R] R'`.
710	-/
711	lemma Algebra.IsPushout.comp_iff {T' : Type*} [CommSemiring T'] [Algebra R T']
712	    [Algebra S' T'] [Algebra S T'] [Algebra T T'] [Algebra R' T']
713	    [IsScalarTower R T T'] [IsScalarTower S T T'] [IsScalarTower S S' T']
714	    [IsScalarTower R R' T'] [IsScalarTower R S' T'] [IsScalarTower R' S' T']
715	    [Algebra.IsPushout R S R' S'] :
716	    Algebra.IsPushout R T R' T' ↔ Algebra.IsPushout S T S' T' := by
717	  let f : R' →ₗ[R] S' := (IsScalarTower.toAlgHom R R' S').toLinearMap
718	  haveI : IsScalarTower R S T' := .of_algebraMap_eq fun x ↦ by
719	    rw [algebraMap_apply R S' T', algebraMap_apply R S S', ← algebraMap_apply S S' T']
720	  have heq : (toAlgHom S S' T').toLinearMap.restrictScalars R ∘ₗ f =
721	      (toAlgHom R R' T').toLinearMap := by
722	    ext x
723	    simp [f, ← IsScalarTower.algebraMap_apply]
724	  rw [isPushout_iff, isPushout_iff, ← heq, IsBaseChange.comp_iff]
725	  exact Algebra.IsPushout.out
726	
727	variable {R R' S S'} in
728	lemma Algebra.IsPushout.of_equiv [h : IsPushout R R' S S']
729	    {T : Type*} [CommSemiring T] [Algebra R' T] [Algebra S T] [Algebra R T]
730	    [IsScalarTower R S T] [IsScalarTower R R' T] (e : S' ≃ₐ[R'] T)
731	    (he : e.toRingHom.comp (algebraMap S S') = algebraMap S T) :
732	    IsPushout R R' S T := by
733	  rw [isPushout_iff] at h ⊢
734	  refine IsBaseChange.of_equiv (h.equiv ≪≫ₗ e.toLinearEquiv) fun x ↦ ?_
735	  simpa [h.equiv_tmul] using DFunLike.congr_fun he x

739	variable (A B : Type*)
740	  [CommRing A] [CommRing B] [Algebra R A] [Algebra R B] [Algebra A B] [Algebra S B]
